channel.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive
channel.description: A daily analysis of all of the AI and global news of the day I find from subscribing to a bunch of interesting newsletters
channel.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
channel.itunes:summary: A daily analysis of all of the AI and global news of the day I find from subscribing to a bunch of interesting newsletters

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 27, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information Dealmaker on boom times for inference providers as Baseten nears a $1 billion raise at $11 billion valuation; The Information Applied AI on Uber COO Andrew Macdonald saying AI lacks proportional ROI after blowing through the year's token budget in months — echoed by Microsoft cutting Claude Code licenses and Starbucks shutting down its AI inventory experiment; The Information AI Agenda on OpenAI's PR challenge as the company navigates safety concerns from former employees and its expansion into advertising; The Information AM on Pope Leo and Anthropic co-founder Dario Amodei both warning of AI power concentration and labor displacement; The Information exclusive on OpenAI targeting smaller advertisers with a new self-serve ads platform; PitchBook on music catalogs striking a chord with private capital as streaming royalties create stable yield instruments; Scott Alexander's Astral Codex Ten on using AI as a voting research assistant for the June 2026 California primary; Ethan Mollick's One Useful Thing on choosing to stay human and the cognitive cost of defaulting to AI; Gary Marcus on the AI bubble risk as Uber, Microsoft, and Starbucks all report disappointing AI ROI; and Lorenzo Thione's weekly AI roundup covering Meta SAM3, Google Antigravity 2.0, the Musk vs. OpenAI verdict, Cursor Composer 2.5, OpenAI solving an 80-year Erdos conjecture, and Anthropic's Colossus compute deal.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: inference providers boom and Baseten's $1B raise; Uber COO says AI lacks ROI as Microsoft cuts Claude Code and Starbucks shuts AI inventory; OpenAI's PR challenge and ad expansion; Pope Leo & Anthropic co-founder warn of AI power concentration; PitchBook on music catalog private capital; Scott Alexander on using AI for voting research; Ethan Mollick on choosing to stay human; Gary Marcus on the AI bubble; and Lorenzo Thione's weekly AI roundup.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 26, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on Twilio's AI-driven stock surge and the double-edged sword of AI cannibalizing its own SMS and communications revenue as Segment acquisition context comes into focus; The Information's The Electric on the Pentagon advocating for low-cost drone and AI military programs as a strategic counterweight to high-cost weapons platforms; The Information on crypto markets and Washington's evolving regulatory posture toward digital assets; and Substack on personal productivity and how to become someone who actually follows through on goals and commitments.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on Twilio's AI boost as a double-edged sword; the Pentagon advocating for low-cost drone & AI programs; crypto Washington dynamics; and Substack on personal productivity and follow-through.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 25, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on SpaceX, Oura, and Blockchain.com's IPO filings as AI-tied stocks like Cerebras (+40%) outperform while last year's tech IPOs have fallen a median 32% — CoreWeave and Circle are the outliers at +160% and +265% — and OpenAI prepares to file confidentially ahead of rival Anthropic both targeting tens of billions in public listings; PitchBook's Weekend Pitch on OpenAI and Anthropic racing to prove their public benefit corporation status means something after Musk's lawsuit was dismissed in 90 minutes — with legal scholars saying the PBC structure changes little in practice; Ignite Insights on the remarkable week where Anthropic is paying rival xAI $1.25 billion per month for Colossus compute (the ultimate customer-landlord-competitor dynamic), Andrej Karpathy joins Anthropic for pre-training, an OpenAI reasoning model solves an 80-year-old Erdos math conjecture confirmed by W.T. Gowers and Thomas Bloom, AI superforecasters match human superforecasters for the first time, Nvidia reports $82B revenue at 85% growth, and Intuit and Meta both cut staff while raising guidance as cities ban data centers over power constraints; Exponential View by Azeem Azhar on why AI token costs keep rising even as per-token prices fall — tokens processed grew 17,000x in four years, agents amplify usage 55x per task, active inference is only 15-20% of actual token consumption, and frontier models cannot accurately forecast their own costs (30x variance across the same task); Passing the Torch Newsletter on Gen Z as one of the biggest mispricings in equity market history, with the peptide and injectable body-modification value chain as the key investable thesis driven by Gen Z's desensitization to cosmetic injectables; and TEN Capital Connect on 28 red flags in startup pitches and the neuromodulation market growing at 9.6% CAGR with over $2.5 billion raised in 2025 led by Neuralink, Synchron, and Inspire Medical.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic IPO race as Oura and Blockchain.com file to go public and AI-tied stocks outperform; PitchBook on OpenAI and Anthropic as public benefit corporations after Musk's lawsuit dismissed in 90 minutes; Ignite Insights on Anthropic paying xAI $1.25B/month for compute, Karpathy joining Anthropic, OpenAI solving an 80-year-old Erdos math problem, Nvidia at $82B revenue, and cities banning data centers; Exponential View on the AI token cost paradox with 17,000x token growth, 55x agent amplification, and 30x cost variance; Gen Z as the biggest equity market mispricing with the peptide value chain as the key investable theme; and TEN Capital Connect on startup pitch red flags and the $2.5B neuromodulation market.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 24, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information Weekend on Stanford ex-president Marc Tessier-Lavigne's complex comeback at Xaira Therapeutics — his $1 billion AI drug discovery startup — three years after resigning amid research misconduct allegations, now pitching AI's ability to identify which patients will benefit from drugs and connecting separate fields of science; The Information on how Alexander Lurie — younger brother of San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie — has become the go-to real estate broker for AI wealth from Anthropic, Apple, Google, OpenAI, and Sequoia as SF luxury home sales hit record highs; The Information on crypto's Washington command center at 1155 F Street as the Blockchain Association and Digital Chamber push the Clarity Act to legitimize crypto in mainstream financial markets; 20 great books for summer 2026 including AI-themed dystopias, San Francisco chronicles, and tech dark comedies; Exponential View #575 by Azeem Azhar on AI's math breakthrough — an OpenAI reasoning model solving an 80-year-old geometry problem by connecting discrete geometry with algebraic number theory in a move reminiscent of AlphaGo's Move 37 — plus AI's creative limits and milquetoast writing; and Azeem Azhar on the growing AI backlash — college graduates booing Eric Schmidt at commencements, AOC showing mud-brown water from Georgia taps near Meta data centers, bipartisan opposition to data centers, and Anthropic turning a profit two years ahead of schedule even as AI's critics grow louder.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
item.itunes:summary: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: Stanford ex-president Marc Tessier-Lavigne's comeback at Xaira Therapeutics AI drug discovery startup; SF's Alexander Lurie as the go-to real estate broker for AI wealth; crypto's Washington command center and the Clarity Act; 20 great summer books; Exponential View on AI solving an 80-year-old math problem; and the growing AI backlash from college campuses to data center construction fights.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 23, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information on San Francisco's tech elite real estate boom with Alexander Lurie — Mayor Daniel Lurie's brother — as the must-know broker for AI wealth from Anthropic, OpenAI, Apple, Google, and Sequoia, with SF median home prices hitting a record $2.15M (up 18% YoY) and luxury home sales at all-time highs; The Information on Microsoft blocking Databricks and Snowflake from connecting data tools to Power BI as it fights to control the "semantic layer" critical for making AI agents more accurate and cheaper to run; The Information exclusive on Chinese battery giant CATL planning to invest in DeepSeek's first-ever $7.35B fundraising round targeting a $51.4B valuation as JD.com and NetEase also join, with CATL betting on future data center power collaborations; The Information AM on OpenAI CEO Sam Altman talking to staff about IPO timing; PitchBook on Oura and the state of healthtech exits; Gary Marcus on the hidden retirement fund risk from AI; AI Snakeoil by Arvind and Sayash asking whether Google's AI agents really built an operating system for $916; DeepLearning.AI The Batch on Hermes vs. OpenClaw, rising cybersecurity alarms, more-interactive AI conversations, and whether agents can truly do human work; and Joseph Gordon-Levitt on AI, filmmaking, and the need for guardrails.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 22, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: OpenAI Q1 revenue of $5.7B led Anthropic by $1B before Anthropic's annualized revenue surged to $45B; Anthropic in talks to use Microsoft's custom Maia 200 AI chips diversifying from Nvidia; OpenAI preparing to file for IPO in coming weeks at $300B valuation; Cursor seeing an opening as GitHub Copilot struggles; AI data centers requiring more batteries as power grids become unsteady; trio of trillion-dollar IPOs in the Dealmaker pipeline; enterprise AI customers negotiating SaaS escape hatches; Scott Alexander's Astral Codex Ten on why new AI paradigms won't save us from capability risks; and Gary Marcus checking the math behind OpenAI and Anthropic's latest revenue headlines.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 20, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: Musk loses OpenAI lawsuit after just two hours of jury deliberation ending the three-week trial — a major victory for Sam Altman; SpaceX IPO expected to return more than $60 billion each to Founders Fund and Valor Equity at a $1.75 trillion valuation, the largest venture returns in history; Microsoft training AI models on 100,000 employees' code via GitHub Copilot and Xbox game studio source code as a competitive moat; the rising costs of frontier AI models driving enterprise customers like Uber and Airbnb to hybrid open-source strategies with Kimi K2.6 and DeepSeek V4; PitchBook on the patent battle in VC secondaries between Nasdaq Private Market and Hiive, plus the end of ARR loans as all 2026 software loans shift to EBITDA-based pricing; Scott Galloway's Prof G Media consolidating on Substack after $20 million in annual podcast revenue and 400,000 newsletter subscribers; Rex Woodbury's Digital Native on Nourish and the decade of consumer health driven by GLP-1 drugs and insurance-covered dietitian access; and Scott Alexander's Astral Codex Ten on California's 60-candidate governor's race and the surreal top-two primary system.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 16, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: Cerebras Systems blockbuster IPO closing up 68% at $311 on first day as the largest semiconductor IPO of all time; Anthropic raising at $900 billion valuation with Dragoneer, Greenoaks, Sequoia, and Altimeter co-leading; Musk vs. OpenAI and Microsoft closing arguments in San Francisco; Valar Atomics nuclear startup building a 40-foot reactor in the Utah desert aiming to achieve criticality by July 4th; PitchBook on AI VC records with defense sector deal count up 382%; US government announcing TRAINS multi-agency task force to evaluate frontier AI models before public release; China blocking Meta's $2.5 billion acquisition of Manus killing the Singapore strategy for Chinese founders; OpenAI's GPT-Realtime-2 speech model with configurable reasoning effort; Google's AI mammogram diagnosis study achieving 54.1% sensitivity vs. human 43.7%; FDA Commissioner Makary resignation and psychedelics research momentum; gaming console price increases driven by memory price quadrupling; and US AI policy patchwork examined by Gary Marcus and Fortune essay co-authors.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 15, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: OpenAI considering legal action against Apple over breach of their ChatGPT partnership deal as Apple grows closer to Google and Anthropic; Cerebras IPO closes at $311/share with Tiger Global returning $1B and Benchmark earning 12x via SPVs; more than 50 researchers and engineers have left SpaceXAI since February as Meta and Thinking Machines hire them away; Anthropic customers like ServiceNow and PagerDuty unable to predict costs due to lack of telemetry data and SLAs; NFX's Pete Flint on how AI expands markets rather than destroying them, using the Kodak Brownie camera analogy; Musk vs. OpenAI trial closing arguments focusing on artificial general intelligence; Astral Codex Ten's Scott Alexander on why sigmoid curves won't necessarily save us from AI capabilities growth; and Exponential View's Azeem Azhar on what Cerebras' IPO pop signals about market understanding of AI inference demand.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 14, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: House Oversight Committee opens investigation into OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's personal investments in Helion and Stoke Space and their ties to OpenAI partnerships, plus 10 GOP attorneys general calling on the SEC to investigate OpenAI's nonprofit-to-for-profit conversion; PitchBook reveals AI startups raised $255.5 billion in Q1 2026 — surpassing all of 2025 — with OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI accounting for 67.3% of all deals as sovereign wealth funds pour into the AI boom; Anthropic triples annualized revenue to $30 billion as enterprise customers like PagerDuty willingly absorb rising Claude costs because productivity gains are too compelling; Anthropic in talks to acquire developer tools startup Stainless for at least $300 million — an SDK maker used by OpenAI and Google — which could force rivals to find new tooling partners; The Information's Clarity Act deep dive on whether crypto's golden moment in Congress will slip away as the Senate Banking Committee prepares to vote this week; Apple secretly exploring how to integrate AI agents into the App Store after months of blocking vibe coding tools, with new Siri powered by Google Gemini managing cross-app tasks; startup Modal raising at a $4.5 billion valuation — an 80% premium from just months ago — after annualized revenue surged 5x to $300 million driven by AI agent sandbox demand from Ramp and Lovable; Exponential View's on-the-ground report from 14 Chinese AI and robotics labs including DeepSeek, MoonshotAI, ByteDance, Unitree, and Galbot — finding that US export controls paradoxically created a more efficient competitor with 3-5x better compute efficiency and a robotics industry years ahead of Western equivalents; and Astral Codex Ten's philosophical deep dive into Nostalgebraist's Hydrogen Jukebox theory of AI taste — why AI writing optimizes for the surface appearance of quality rather than quality itself, and what that means for the era of AI-generated content.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 13, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information exclusive on former Alibaba star researcher Lin Wei launching a new AI lab seeking a $2 billion valuation, with backing from top Chinese investors; Anthropic in talks to acquire developer tools startup Sketch — used by both OpenAI and Google — signaling Anthropic's push into the enterprise software stack beyond just model APIs; Microsoft generating over $30 billion in revenue from OpenAI's technology while managing a complex financial relationship where spending and revenue are tightly intertwined; OpenAI making billions just by promising to buy from suppliers — a strategy of using purchase commitments as a fundraising instrument that has implications for the entire AI compute ecosystem; The Humanly Possible Substack on the supercharging of art and creativity through AI, examining how generative tools are transforming artistic practice and what that means for human creative identity; Paul O'Brien on Houston's emerging startup ecosystem and why the Bayou City is becoming a serious contender in the US technology landscape with unique advantages in energy, healthcare, and space; and the broader industrialization of AI infrastructure as the race to build compute at civilizational scale accelerates.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 12, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: The Information exclusive on OpenAI saving $97 billion through 2030 in its renegotiated Microsoft revenue-sharing deal, with payments capped at $38 billion — boosting IPO prospects but increasing short-term cash burn; Kuaishou planning to spin off its Kling AI video unit at a $20 billion valuation ahead of a 2027 IPO, as Chinese AI companies rush to tap public markets; Anthropic signing a $1.8 billion cloud deal with Akamai, diversifying away from hyperscaler dependence; PitchBook on the $500 million North Carolina quartz mine holding up the world's AI chip supply chain — a single Spruce Pine facility produces 90% of the world's ultra-high-purity quartz critical for semiconductor fabs; China's battery swapping boom as NIO's network hits 4 million swaps per day and BYD, CATL, and Geely build competing standards; Andrew Yang's warning that AI is already destroying jobs at scale with data pointing to structural unemployment for white-collar workers; DeepLearning.AI The Batch on the latest AI research breakthroughs; Moore's Law economics and how cost-per-token improvements are reshaping the AI competitive landscape; and venture capital funding rounds and startup news from across the AI ecosystem.

item.title: Lorenzo's Daily Deep Dive - May 8, 2026
item.description: Today's AI and global news roundup covering: OpenAI's custom AI chip program "Nexus" with Broadcom hits an $18 billion financing snag as Microsoft hasn't committed to buying 40% of the chips — with the full 10 GW program potentially costing $180 billion; fast-follow VC rounds accelerating as Core Automation (ex-OpenAI researcher Jerry Tworek's six-week-old startup) targets a $4 billion valuation weeks after raising at $1 billion, fueled by Nvidia acting as a central bank for neolab AI startups; Allen Control Systems, maker of the Bullfrog autonomous anti-drone weapon station, targeting a $2 billion valuation up from $140M — a 14x increase — as defense tech VC deals surge to $50 billion; NFX's Pete Flint on Anthropic's "Project Deal" experiment where 69 employees used AI agents to negotiate $4,000 in real transactions, previewing a world where agents own the marketplace; Astral Codex Ten's Scott Alexander on three model organisms for taste — Reddit vexillology flag design wars, Star Wars plot holes, and why tech company names like "Infinita" feel like manipulative easy wins; and the Sinica podcast on whether Vietnam is drifting closer to China, with scholar Khang Vu arguing the "Four No's" and non-aligned foreign policy remain bedrock despite growing economic ties with Beijing.
item.itunes:author: Lorenzo Thione
